publications.title: Structure of the complex between human T-cell receptor, viral peptide and HLA-A2.
publications.authors: Garboczi DN, Ghosh P, Utz U, Fan QR, Biddison WE, Wiley DC.
publications.executive_summary: This work presents the first crystal structure of a human TCR bound to a viral peptide presented on HLA-A2, establishing the molecular architecture of MHC-I recognition. The TCR engages the peptide–MHC complex through its variable loops, which contact nearly the entire peptide surface while also engaging a substantial area of the MHC molecule itself, and adopts a diagonal orientation across the peptide-binding groove that appears conserved across both class I and class II MHC molecules. This general binding geometry has profound implications for understanding how the immune repertoire is selected during development and explains the structural basis for alloreactivity—the ability of TCRs to cross-react with peptides presented on different MHC alleles.

publications.title: Four A6-TCR/peptide/HLA-A2 structures that generate very different T cell signals are nearly identical.
publications.authors: Ding YH, Baker BM, Garboczi DN, Biddison WE, Wiley DC.
publications.executive_summary: The A6 T cell receptor was crystallized bound to HLA-A2 presenting the HTLV-1 Tax peptide and three single-residue variants (P6A, V7R, Y8A) that range from weak agonists to antagonists and exhibit different TCR affinities. Despite generating markedly different T cell signaling outcomes—from agonism to antagonism—the four complex structures are nearly superimposable, with only minor local adjustments at the TCR/peptide/MHC interface to accommodate the amino acid substitutions. This striking structural similarity across functionally distinct ligands provides direct crystallographic evidence that TCR signal outcome (activation vs. antagonism) is not determined by gross conformational rearrangement of the TCR itself, challenging the prevailing model that ligand-induced TCR remodeling drives differential signaling. The findings are relevant to understanding TCR recognition specificity and how small changes in peptide sequence can alter immunological outcomes without requiring detectable changes in receptor geometry, with implications for autoimmunity and viral infection where variant epitopes may behave as agonists or antagonists despite structural similarity to natural ligands.

publications.title: T cell receptor recognition via cooperative conformational plasticity.
publications.authors: Gagnon SJ, Borbulevych OY, Davis-Harrison RL, Turner RV, Damirjian M, Wojnarowicz A, Biddison WE, Baker BM.
publications.executive_summary: The study examined the HTLV-1 Tax-specific TCR A6 and its cross-reactive recognition of a hapten-modified Tax peptide (Tax-5K-4-(3-Indolyl)-butyric acid) presented by HLA-A*0201. Crystal structures revealed that binding occurs through cooperative conformational plasticity, involving simultaneous conformational adjustments on both the TCR side (CDR loops and Valpha/Vbeta domain reorientation) and the peptide-MHC side (hapten-modified peptide). This finding demonstrates that TCR cross-reactivity—a mechanism implicated in autoimmune disease—operates through complex and dynamic protein rearrangements at the binding interface rather than simple epitope mimicry or isolated loop movements. The results suggest that the intrinsic flexibility of both TCR and peptide-MHC molecules enables recognition of structurally diverse ligands, offering insights into how TCRs achieve broad specificity while maintaining functional discrimination.

publications.title: Fluorine substitutions in an antigenic peptide selectively modulate T-cell receptor binding in a minimally perturbing manner.
publications.authors: Piepenbrink KH, Borbulevych OY, Sommese RF, Clemens J, Armstrong KM, Desmond C, Do P, Baker BM.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined how fluorine substitutions in the HTLV-1 Tax(11-19) peptide presented on MHC selectively modulate recognition by two different Tax-specific TCRs (A6 and B7). Using crystallographic structures and structure-guided design, the authors found that fluorination enhanced binding by TCR A6 while weakening binding by TCR B7, with minimal perturbation to peptide structure. The selectivity arises from a "polar hydrophobicity" mechanism of fluorine's effect on TCR recognition rather than purely hydrophobic or electrostatic interactions. This approach of rational fluorine substitution offers a tool for enhancing TCR-peptide-MHC interactions in a discriminating manner, with potential applications in vaccine development and overcoming the inherent weak, fast kinetics that challenge structural studies of TCR recognition.

publications.title: T cell receptor cross-reactivity directed by antigen-dependent tuning of peptide-MHC molecular flexibility.
publications.authors: Borbulevych OY, Piepenbrink KH, Gloor BE, Scott DR, Sommese RF, Cole DK, Sewell AK, Baker BM.
publications.executive_summary: The A6 TCR recognizes two structurally similar peptides—Tax (LLFGYPVYV) and Tel1p (MLWGYLQYV)—both presented by HLA-A2, despite forming substantially different interfaces with the receptor. Conformational differences arise not only in the peptides and TCR but also unexpectedly in the MHC molecule itself, with peptide-dependent modulation of HLA-A2 dynamics distinguishing the two complexes; these differences are undetectable in free pMHC structures and cannot be explained by classical induced-fit binding. This work reveals that differential "tuning" of MHC molecular flexibility by different peptide ligands—rather than static structural mimicry alone—underlies TCR cross-reactivity, fundamentally altering our understanding of how the immune system accommodates structural diversity and recognizes related antigens. The findings have implications for TCR engineering and predicting cross-reactive responses in both viral infection (Tax from HTLV-1) and other contexts where TCR recognition of multiple peptides is biologically important.

publications.title: Conformational melding permits a conserved binding geometry in TCR recognition of foreign and self molecular mimics.
publications.authors: Borbulevych OY, Piepenbrink KH, Baker BM.
publications.executive_summary: The αβ TCR A6 recognizes both the foreign antigen Tax from human T cell leukemia virus-1 (HTLV-1) and a self peptide from the neuronal protein HuD, both presented by HLA-A2. Although the HuD and Tax epitopes are structural mimics rather than perfect matches, A6 engages both peptides using an identical, conserved binding mode despite requiring conformational adjustments in the peptide and MHC for HuD recognition. This molecular mimicry between viral and self antigens provides insight into how cross-reactivity can occur and may explain the development of HTLV-1-associated myelopathy/tropical spastic paraparesis as an autoimmune complication. The study shows that low stability of the HuD–HLA-A2 complex permits survival of self-reactive T cells bearing this TCR while maintaining affinity levels consistent with negative selection, demonstrating how conformational flexibility in pMHC complexes can enable molecular mimicry-driven autoimmunity.

publications.title: Disparate degrees of hypervariable loop flexibility control T-cell receptor cross-reactivity, specificity, and binding mechanism.
publications.authors: Scott DR, Borbulevych OY, Piepenbrink KH, Corcelli SA, Baker BM.
publications.executive_summary: The A6 αβ TCR was studied using crystallography, spectroscopy, and computation to understand how flexibility of complementarity-determining region (CDR) loops controls recognition of multiple antigenic peptides presented by MHC-I. The key finding is that CDR3β and CDR3α hypervariable loops have markedly different energy landscapes: CDR3β displays a broad, smooth landscape enabling rapid sampling of multiple conformations compatible with diverse ligands, while CDR3α has a rugged landscape constraining sampling to fewer conformations, thereby promoting specificity for particular peptides and MHC proteins. This differential loop flexibility directly explains TCR cross-reactivity and specificity at the molecular level, revealing that TCR binding employs a hybrid mechanism incorporating both conformational selection and induced fit. The work establishes loop dynamics as a fundamental determinant of TCR recognition breadth versus narrowness, with implications for understanding how a limited TCR repertoire achieves both functional cross-reactivity and the selectivity required to distinguish self from non-self.
